title: From Corporate Executive to Sound Healer: One Woman's Journey Through Grief
guestBio: Marcie Dean is the founder of LovEvolve Music and CEO of Electric Blue Communications. After 18 years at IBM and serving as Creative Director on Watson AIOps at AWS, she discovered her gift for creating frequency-based healing music following profound personal loss.
excerpt: When Marcie Dean lost her partner, brother, and corporate career within six months, she opened GarageBand and started channeling healing music with zero training. Now she's pioneering Spirit Tech to help regulate nervous systems through sound.
metaDescription: Former AWS executive Marcie Dean shares how devastating loss led her to discover frequency healing music and build a business channeling sound therapy.
keyTakeaways: Grief encompasses losing career identity, relationships, and the life you thought you'd have, not just people.
Your nervous system has a unique frequency signature that requires personalized healing approaches.
Mindset strategies cannot override biology - nervous system regulation must come first.
Surrender is not giving up but rather stopping the fight against what already exists.
The digital world cuts us off from natural healing frequencies our bodies desperately need.
Custom frequency music can reach neurodivergent individuals in ways traditional therapy cannot.
Sometimes the biggest career pivots come from following signs and synchronicities rather than strategic plans.

pullQuotes.text: Surrender is not giving up — it's stopping the fight against what already is
Your nervous system is as unique as your personality
Mindset work alone can't fix a chronically dysregulated nervous system

timestamps.label: 18 years at IBM and the AWS pivot
Father's Day 2023 — what happened on the boat
Grief as career and identity loss
Surrender vs struggle — the birth analogy
Advice to women called to a new chapter

quiz.title: What's Your Healing Leadership Style?
quiz.description: Discover how you naturally navigate challenges and support others through your unique approach to healing and growth.

A.name: The Intuitive Pioneer
A.description: You lead with your inner knowing and aren't afraid to venture into uncharted territory. Like Marcie, you're willing to follow signs and synchronicities even when they don't make logical sense. Your gift lies in sensing what's trying to emerge and trusting the process even when others don't understand. This episode will inspire you to trust your intuitive gifts even more deeply and show you how surrender can be your greatest strength.

B.name: The Compassionate Bridge
B.description: You have a natural ability to translate healing concepts into practical applications that others can understand and implement. You see the connections between different approaches and help people find what works for their unique situation. Your strength is making the mystical accessible and the complex simple. Marcie's journey from corporate to sound healing will resonate with your ability to bridge different worlds and find practical applications for transformative work.

C.name: The Gentle Warrior
C.description: You approach healing and leadership with both strength and tenderness, understanding that true power comes from vulnerability and authentic connection. You're not afraid of difficult emotions or challenging conversations because you know that's where the real growth happens. Your gift is holding space for others' pain while maintaining hope for their healing. This episode will validate your approach and show you how grief can become a pathway to your greatest gifts.

D.name: The Systematic Healer
D.description: You believe in the power of structure and proven methods to create lasting change. Like Marcie's scientific approach to frequency healing, you appreciate when spiritual concepts are backed by research and measurable results. Your strength lies in creating reproducible systems that help people heal consistently. This episode will show you how to honor both your analytical nature and your deeper calling to serve others through healing work.

questions.question: When facing a major life transition, you're most likely to:

options.text: Trust your gut feeling even if you can't explain the logic behind your choices
Research different approaches and find a middle path that incorporates the best of each
Allow yourself to feel all the emotions fully while staying open to what wants to emerge
Create a structured plan with clear steps and measurable milestones

questions.question: When someone comes to you for support during a difficult time, you naturally:

options.text: Share your own intuitive insights and encourage them to listen to their inner wisdom
Help them see connections they might have missed and offer multiple perspectives
Hold space for their pain without trying to fix it, trusting their healing process
Provide practical tools and resources that have proven effective for similar situations

questions.question: Your relationship with uncertainty and the unknown is:

options.text: Exciting - you see uncertainty as an invitation to discover new possibilities
Manageable - you can work with uncertainty by finding familiar elements within it
Accepting - you've learned that uncertainty often precedes your greatest growth
Challenging but workable - you prefer to create structure within uncertain situations

questions.question: When you think about combining spirituality with practical work, you feel:

options.text: Energized - this integration feels like your natural way of being in the world
Curious - you love exploring how different approaches can complement each other
Peaceful - you believe authentic work must honor both the practical and sacred aspects of life
Interested - especially when there's evidence or measurable results to support the approach

questions.question: Your approach to healing (yourself or others) tends to be:

options.text: Experimental and intuitive, following what feels right in the moment
Integrative, drawing from multiple modalities and traditions
Patient and gentle, honoring the natural timing of healing processes
Methodical and evidence-based, preferring approaches with proven track records

questions.question: If you were to leave your current career for something completely different, you would:

options.text: Follow the signs and synchronicities, trusting that the path will reveal itself
Find ways to use your existing skills in service of your new calling
Allow the transition to unfold naturally, without forcing a timeline
Create a transition plan that minimizes risk while maximizing learning opportunities

transcript:
**Aimee:** Today's guest is someone with a story that's very different from most of the conversations we have on this show. But in a way, I think it will resonate with so many women who have ever gone through loss, reinvention, and the challenge of building something new when life doesn't go according to plan.

I'm joined today by Marcie Dean, CEO of Electric Blue Communications and the founder and visionary behind Love Evolve Music. She's a former corporate tech leader with a background at companies like IBM and AWS, where she worked at the intersection of technology, user experience, and innovation. But Marcie's story took a dramatic turn after profound personal loss and a major professional transition. What emerged on the other side was a new creative path. One that blends technology, sound, healing, entrepreneurship, and a very different way of thinking about how people process emotion, stress, and transformation.

What I find especially interesting about Marcie is that she is not coming at this from a purely artistic background. She comes from systems, structure, product thinking, human centered design, and big tech leadership. And now she's applying that same mindset to music healing experiences and building something that does not fit neatly into a traditional box.

So, in today's conversation, we're talking about what happens when your life blows up when the corporate path no longer fits and when you feel called to build something unconventional. We will talk about grief, reinvention, creativity, nervous system support, entrepreneurship, and what it looks like to trust a new path even when you can't fully explain it yet. Marcie, welcome to The Manager.

**Marcie:** Yay! Thank you, Aimee. So happy to be here. Honestly, been looking forward to this one.

**Aimee:** Me, too. So, you have such an interesting background because you spent years in corporate tech at such a high level before moving into the work that you're doing now. Could you start by giving us the arc of your professional story? What your life looked like before this major pivot?

**Marcie:** Absolutely. I mean, the cool thing is even though I was in tech, I was creative my entire life. My parents were both into music and my mom was an artist and I was pushed in different directions, but I always went for this design route, this art route, and knew exactly what I was going to do.

Went to school for graphic design and then I got picked up by IBM. Ended up spending 18 years there starting as a basic UX designer, visual designer and then that evolved into different team leadership and then management senior management roles for some big name products that they had.

And then during the COVID time period, interestingly enough, it opened up the floodgates for companies to hire without worrying about location. So I was getting hit up by recruiter after recruiter. Basically narrowed it down to offers from Amazon Web Services which is AWS. A lot of people asked me what is that and then Google and I made this choice to go to AWS.

It was a great package and it was a senior management level role. People warned me it's difficult at AWS and I thought no I'm good at getting my job done in less time than most people do. It was culture and so that was an interesting shift for me. But in that same time period, I left a marriage that was not healthy for either of us or our kids. But, you know, you keep trying to hang in there. That's basically what leads up to the next chapter of my life of what has suddenly shifted since then.

**Aimee:** Wild. So, yeah. So, a lot of the women who are listening are in some kind of transition, career, identity, grief, or entrepreneurship. So, this wasn't just a career pivot for you. It was also connected to a deep personal loss. Would you mind sharing your story with us? What happened that changed it all?

**Marcie:** Absolutely. So, I actually met a once in a lifetime soulmate after leaving that marriage. I moved into the house that was something I had drawn as a kid. It's my dream home. It's not something huge fancy, but it's just this unique house. And I'm meeting this person. I'm thinking, "Oh, everything's finally coming together because I'm making shifts in my life with the career and the marriage and all that."

And he and I had this really deep soul level connection, but I started having fears that something was going to happen to him and I talked to him about it. And so we talked about things. We started living life like every day was his last, right? We just wanted to really embrace that because I was hoping it wasn't fear. But I was a little OCD and I'm like, "Let's get your health checked out, all that."

But about 6 and a half months in, actually, he moved in with me. He had this beautiful son. I had my daughters. I thought this was the next chapter of my life. And Father's Day weekend of 2023, we went to go boating with friends and that was always another thing for me. My dad had passed 10 years prior. He was a boat captain, so being on the water felt absolutely divinely guided, but he did not come back alive.

We were out there and there's a private island outside of North Carolina, Wilmington area and you can dock on the sound side and it's beautiful. People have all their picnic gear out, kids are playing and then on the other side it's an unobscured beach and I happened to be sitting back on the boat after lunch when we all been on the sound side when everyone else went the other direction. I thought that John, my partner, was going to come back by after he took his son to find place to use the bathroom and I'm waiting, waiting, waiting and I'm finally like, "Okay, what's going on?"

So I go around to the aftermath where my daughter comes running to me and tells me something happened to John and I see people doing chest compressions on him. I went through a whole blurry point of everything at that point. I can't tell you what happened between that and me being somehow boated to the mainland with his son, but he saved his son's life in a rip current and he did not make it.

And then after that, just to tie into the career part, is that I took a grief leave, which I was a little resistant because I thought maybe if I just get back to work, I'll be okay. But I still felt this connection to him and because we had talked about this. He always made the comment if anything ever happens to me, I'll communicate with you through music. We bonded over music stuff that my ex-husband and his ex-wife weren't really always aligned on, but we were like, "Oh, remember this band from back in the day." And so I was like, "Great. Please Spotify, playlist me, whatever you want to send."

But when I returned from grief leave, I was feeling this deeper connection, this deeper pull to do something different. And I was laid off because there's a new return to office policy. And if you know anything about what's going on with corporate these days, now we add AI into the mix, but there's lots of shifts. And only a month after that, my brother actually passed of a fatal overdose. And so it was like boom, boom, boom. Less than 6 months, my entire life changed.

And that made me foreign to a lot of my friends. They didn't even know what to do for me. People I had known for years, family members, everything started to disintegrate in different directions. And you know, at the same time, I was feeling very divinely led into audio work. And I'm like, yeah, my parents tried to get me to play flute and piano, and I was like, no, I don't want to learn this. I loved music, but trying to learn to read sheet music or anything was an absolute no.

One day, I was just guided, make a song. And I'm like, oh, there's a thing called Garage Band on my computer that I've never used. So, I did. And meanwhile, I've met this other wonderful man who was one of the first people who understood what I was going through. And he was just a friend to me. He helped me out with stuff around the house that John hadn't taken care of. He's actually got a music background. He's like, "Marcie, I don't know what you're doing, but you're doing it all backwards, breaking production rules." I'm like, "I'm just being guided."

So, next thing I know, I'm making this music. And two years plus now, I've made over 400 tracks and no experience. And it's wild how much my entire life shifted.

**Aimee:** That's awesome. So, do you think that grief strips away performance and forces something more honest?

**Marcie:** It absolutely does. And then it's hard that we're still living in the systems that say you've got to pay your bills, you got to do these things and these things and these things. And to pioneer something so different because what I did discover by not going and reading the rules of audio technology is a way to bring in different frequencies that it's a proprietary process that's patentable that at first I thought this was just a grief gift. I still thought I'll keep applying to corporate jobs. I'll go back. That's been always pushed away. You're not going that way is all I get.

But the more I shared my music, the more I connected with people online that were also in this journey of awakening to their true purpose, people who were entrepreneurs. Again, I didn't even understand entrepreneurship. I was the soccer mom. I was the corporate leader and I was the everyone knows me as this mainstream Marcie. I've always been empathic. I've always been tapped in, but I wasn't ever into any kind of out there stuff. And I'm not building healing tracks or doing woo things.

So I was sharing this music and then the responses from other people were so profound that I was blown away being that I've come from user experience. I gave a lot away free upfront but I'm very thankful I did because when you're pioneering something new I was discovering and then researching. It wasn't I went out to build a business. I'm suddenly realizing oh wait I'm an entrepreneur now. This was not something Marcie consciously chose.

So just seeing the impact and going with it has been totally out of the box. I was always a little bit of out of the box thinker, but never would I have if you told me five years ago that this is what I'd be doing with my life, I would have laughed, right? This is not at all what I believed.

**Aimee:** So you're an accidental entrepreneur then.

**Marcie:** Absolutely.

**Aimee:** And now you're creating music and sound experiences that can support people emotionally.

**Marcie:** Emotionally, physically. I mean, it's been, and this is where the testing continues to go on and different people are impacted in different ways. Some of my adversaries are musicians who are like, "That doesn't sound right cuz it doesn't go the way my partner, for example, who's like that's not how production works." But he's like, "If I had stepped in your way and told you how to do it, you wouldn't have discovered what you did. You wouldn't have perfected what you did."

So, I've spent 5,000 hours just completely dedicated to divine guidance and to building these tracks a certain way. And I've had people that are healing from trauma, people who have substituted it for addiction problems, they can tap into this music and I say if people listen to it as medicine as in do it regularly, not just one-off, but within 5 minutes most people feel something from it, but when they use it regularly, it helps with their emotional and mental state.

A lot of people are like, I'm envisioning things for my life. I'm seeing myself ice skating. I'm like, it takes them to a place that is outside of the normal overthinking brain. So a big majority of the people end up being neurodivergent overthinkers anxiety-ridden which is what I suffered from where I'm like this music was saving me. The creation process is a whole other thing but just the impact of the frequency work and the layers and the way that I've been guided to build it, it's not a typical sound bath, it's not spa meditation music, it's genre bending out there really interesting stuff.

**Aimee:** It really is. Well you know I actually found you on Threads and I think I saw a post that was something about frequency music and I was like well that's cool because you know do you on social media you see a lot of stuff about frequency generators. I have a Beexley I have a Schuman I love them you know I play with earth frequency lately I'm playing with a 432 which is a frequency that's really good for sleep.

**Marcie:** And a heart frequency.

**Aimee:** It's the love frequency yeah and I've been sleeping with it the last few nights and I really have been sleeping super good. And so I'm understanding that there you know basically frequency goes in hertz and basically goes from zero to what a thousand or something.

**Marcie:** The interesting part is again me discovering music and how production works there's a lot of things that are capped off. And what I've learned is that in our digital tech world that we're living in now, unless you're actually going to a sound bath or you're out there doing tribal drumming or you're in nature all the time, in the digital world, we're actually being suffocated from we're being cut off from the actual healing frequencies that can exist.

So when I say I was breaking all the production rules, I was blasting everything because I felt this connection to John. His promise to communicate through music I did not believe would be oh you're gonna be a musician now and you're gonna but no this connection went on and being able to tap into that level of spiritual awareness and bring frequencies through by doing things that might hurt other people's ears at first but then I'm doing this extra processing where I'm treating instrumental layers like voice.

So I'm bringing back what sounds like a root language, what sounds like a foreign language. And the interesting part is is with the language layered with the music and the way that I build it overrides that analytical brain, that brain that doesn't want to shut off because you feel like you can even look up research about listening to foreign music that you don't understand versus the lyrics that get stuck in your head where you know you can't get that bop out of your head. That's a very different thing.

And so it's again everything I've done is go ahead and do it and then go research why it's working. And it's been such a crazy journey.

**Aimee:** That's so wild. So what first made you realize that sound and music might be more than entertainment that they could actually support healing or emotional processing?

**Marcie:** Well, again, so when I first discovered this and I'm being pushed, I was like, this is just another thankful God-given gift that I have. But it was the responses that other people were having from it and the fact that I had chronic anxiety. I've lived with anxiety my entire life and now I've gone through severe grief and I talk about grief with people and I coach people through grief. It's not just somebody that you've lost which I have gone through that but the career identity and that shift that was the longest relationship I've ever had. All these things equate to that.

And in spite of all that, where I might have freaked out over the littlest thing, I didn't. I freaked out as a kid if I turned in a paper late or I got a bad grade. Now I've gone through serious loss, including my corporate identity. And when I was building this music, I felt a calm no matter what was going on.

And it's really hard to be there. And there's still I'm on a human existence plane and I have a roller coaster of emotions and I have days that are harder than others. But throughout all of this, this music, this gift was helping me stay stable and calm in situations that I'm like, I shouldn't be calm in this situation. And so, again, just continuing to research that and connect with other people that have been into sound healing, that have done more research on more of the analog versions of this, it's just been wild to actually understand. They're doing tons of research on how they use certain frequencies for cancer treatments and things that are not always publicly knowledgeable. It's a thing.

**Aimee:** Yeah, I mean I was familiar with the Rife machine which is really old school and I know he was using it to treat parasites and different pathogens and cancer and all kinds of stuff back in the olden days and it's a frequency generator basically that you hold in your hands. It's interesting that frequency seems to be part of the bigger picture for the future of healing. It seems like this might be on some level, some sort of the future of medicine, you know, it definitely seems like it's another modality that can heal humanity on some level.

**Marcie:** Absolutely. And that's actually one of the things that again just from my own learning, I wasn't guided to go chase down all the current research before I discover something and I ask why. I ask how. I do that research. But I kind of have a theory that again we all have energy bodies. We're energy. Everyone knows no matter what you believe in that energy cannot be created nor destroyed all that.

So I feel like we all have a unique frequency signature and the more I've worked with clients building custom tracks. What's wild is I am tapping into this intuitive flow and I'll build a custom track for people and what comes through in the building sometimes the most calm guy ever that was loving my music.

**Marcie:** I made a track for him and it came back like some high-speed EDM. So when I say this is not just mellow spa music, it's not. It's the widest range of music, he was like "this is exactly what my nervous system needs" and I was in shock. I questioned it at first, but I just again trust my intuition.

I've realized our nervous systems are as unique as our personalities and they don't always match. So what someone may come across like, that's not necessarily what their nervous system needs. You might seem really calm and you need some high-speed things to keep you there, and other people may seem like they're all over the place and they need something more mellow. And so I literally can basically build anything.

I'm not a fan of country music and I still ended up building something that sounded a little twangy for one of my clients and she looked a little punk rock. So I was like again going "I don't know if this is right for you." She's like "I love country" but that's about where my limits go. But it's just been a beautiful journey to actually just kind of tap into what somebody's needs are.

And then not only have I elevated and kind of flipped the frequency range up to a point where if our physical devices have a control range, you can only turn your computer up to a certain thing or a TV up to a certain volume level. I can play my music with the way I've shifted it at like an 11 when you need it at 100 to listen to the TV show. So there's a lot that I'm learning beyond even the work that I'm doing for individuals just about how we have honestly ended up in this very digital world that things are being compressed in a way and shut down in a way that we're not getting that natural healing.

**Aimee:** Yeah, I mean that's one of the reasons I have the frequency generator in my room is because humans used to sleep on the earth and there wasn't electricity and there weren't cell phone towers and Wi-Fi. And so our bodies are used to this frequency. What is it? 7.32 or something like that. That's Earth. And so your body is now being interrupted by frequencies that are not the original OG frequencies that human bodies evolved with. Right? So I'm glad and I even understand when we made the shift with music production music, it used to be in a different frequency and they actually shifted it.

**Marcie:** Yeah. Yeah. That was the first phase of it all. And I've heard different stories, different people. Again, I feel like history, everything I've learned from this is there's a lot of things that we were lied to about, different versions of whatever stories and depending on what history book you read or what encyclopedias you had at one point, you just got to take whatever they say. But yes, there was a transition during the Hitler phase of things.

**Aimee:** That's right.

**Marcie:** They shifted things. And again, this is all my learning after the fact, but I feel like what I've learned is we as humans have as souls, whatever, we have a very specific frequency signature. So imagine like pi, it could go on forever or your own phone number, your own everything. And within our voice, there's a lot of stuff I've done with understanding that you can get a read on how your body is doing, how you're aligned in certain ways just by a recording of your voice.

And when I remember John because I spent so much time with him in this tapped in phase, I can I could hear him still. So I could feel his energy and feel that out in different sounds even if no one else heard it. It's like you can hear everyone that you love. You can connect to everyone you love and everyone has a very unique frequency signature.

So I call it like a radio. When you're not aligned with whatever you're supposed to be doing, when you feel off or anything, it's like you can just tune the dial a little bit to kind of get to the right station you want to be on for your life to go in a completely different way. And some days when we're feeling off, we're in that static, right? So, it's certain ways of intentionally tuning yourself in to what you want for your life and what you want for that day is really big.

**Aimee:** Yeah. Totally. Almost like harmonizing our signature. So sometimes we can get thrown off our frequency can get thrown off by sadness or grief or it could be something that may upset us but it's resetting basically reharmonizing the nervous system so that we can be in the frequency that we're supposed to be in optimal frequency range for our unique signature.

**Marcie:** And that's one of the cool things with the music that I've used it within team collaborative meetings or with practitioners, there's a way to align frequencies with who you're talking to. So, I don't know if anyone works corporate still or is in one of those meetings where you're like, "I am just not picking up on what's going on here." And I mean, maybe you're in just a bad meeting because those happen too, but many people come in from their previous engagement, jump right into something, and everyone's off and how many people are actually listening or just trying to chime in to answer.

So, I would use this with different teams that I worked with and other practitioners as a level setting tool. So we'd listen to a track at the beginning and you just watch everyone come in more calm. We've just aligned our frequency by using the frequencies in this music to be on the same page and then the conversation would be so much more fluid which was one of the coolest things that I came out of because I don't know how many times in meetings jumped in life and work and I'd be so stressed and not be able to tap in.

**Aimee:** Well whatever we can do as humans to help us communicate better because I feel like spoken language is one of our biggest limitations as beings on the earth right now and I feel like poor communication can lead to war and all kinds of horrific things and it's the more we can perfect communication with one another the better off we will be as a species.

**Marcie:** And so that's where when I was kind of guided to build a business before I made Love Evolve Music. It was Electric Blue Communications and that actually is about extending communication again, people who are going through grief and there are a lot of people are like "I just want to sign. I've heard other people have signs from their loved ones." I was tapped in enough to John's to get those signs and see them clearly and I've actually been able to help people through the grief to tap into that as well.

But it's about the fact that we are such a mess because of misinterpretation of language. So, we're speaking the same language, but the way that we receive, especially how many times a text message comes through and whatever you're feeling towards that person, you feel something. I've told people so many times, if you're feeling emotions towards anyone, even if they are typically bad mouthing you or a bad person, go back, put the phone down, go back and read it as if it is from your best friend, and see if you don't see something different because I think so many fights happen that way.

And then we have this ever growing autism spectrum, non-verbal kids, these things that are just more prevalent these days. And there's a huge disconnect on how do we understand them? And the more we can actually tap into the non-verbal cues, the energetic cues, the better our entire existence would be, right? I know it's not always easy, but it's definitely important.

**Aimee:** It's been amazing. That's one of the things that you've been kind of moving towards in your work is kind of creating a bridge for families with kids who are neurodivergent using frequency music to do that. What's been some of your experience with that? What have you seen?

**Marcie:** Yeah. So, so as I said, so John saved his son's life and his son was actually on the autism spectrum. This kid was verbal and he was very smart and he was very emotionally intelligent, but he would kind of talk in puzzling ways and he just said certain things where I was like I hadn't spent that much time around someone with a formal autism diagnosis where I had such a passion and love for his son that I was like, "John, this kid's not autistic. He's a psychic. He just he has these profound statements and he only says what really matters. He's not going to get into the weeds of all the side talk that most of us do." He just really had profound things to say.

In this whole journey, I felt like we were guided through this purpose that we have to help bridge the gap to help the adults heal and break the generational curses that we're all here trying to break. And we don't want to repeat our parental cycles and their parental cycles. And these kids are wired in a way for spirit almost where they can't repeat those patterns because they're not wired that way. But then there's this big gap of how they don't fit into our societal norms, the school systems that are still going on like they were in the 50s. My I've got teenage daughters and I'm like they still have you do all that. There's no shift for as much as we've shifted.

And so I've worked with a few parents with non-verbal kids specifically and some others that are verbal but on the autism spectrum. And I help them by creating custom tracks and I let the kid choose and the parent is there. So if I'm not getting the perfect non-verbal cues, I typically can because I've been more tapped into that. Even over a Zoom call, they select certain sounds and I can see if they are feeling them or not. So they're selecting the sound base that is resonant with their nervous system.

And then I go build this custom track. I do all my mastering and frequency filtering. And then again, this root language that's woven through it allows them to just kind of shut down all the stimulus that's around them typically. And it helps adults even tap into more of their intuition or deeper levels of consciousness. But what I've learned is that these kids light up like somebody's finally speaking their language instead of a parent going, "You need to say that the right way. Please speak. Please speak. Please speak." The pressure that's put on the kids that are already struggling.

It's like you come into a language where you probably comprehend something so much bigger and different that it feels like if I was like "Amy, let's just speak in three-letter words for the rest of this conversation." How far does the conversation go? So, they shut down, right? And so, these kids' eyes have lit up. I've heard behavioral shifts have happened. Kids have just in panic modes been able to come to slow down, calm down. Even kids that are supposedly sensitive with audio and they can't handle intense audio. They've been able to put on headphones and my the parents are like, "I don't know, my girls never ever listened with headphones. You normally push them off," but they tap in and they light up. I've just had some beautiful experiences so far.

And so I've recently piloted a new program where now that I've gotten enough user research from two years working with adults and a few kids, I'm opening up this program where they get to create and co-create with me their own custom track and use it as a tool that they have anytime they need.

**Aimee:** Yeah, you shared with me a beautiful video of a young man with a pretty severe case of neurodivergence and he had never really spoken before and he listened to your track. He was laying on a couch and his mom played him a track and he said, "I love it." And that's pretty amazing.

**Marcie:** 13-year-old, mind you. And so she's like, "He's outgrowing me. He's taller than me now. Look at all these recipes we can come up with with what's in here. So, I would love to help.

**Aimee:** That is so cool. I think we just have to give people one good experience and then you get one little win and then you're like, "Okay, well, what else can I do with this?" So, I'd love to do that with you, Marcie. Let's do that for sure.

**Marcie:** That would be amazing. I mean, I've tested a lot of things, too. So, if I can save you from going to the wrong tools for the wrong things, that's another one, right? Let my failures lead the way. And I'll be like, "Don't do this. You want this, go there." So, yeah, let's definitely set that up, Aimee.

**Aimee:** So, let's do that. Okay. Well, we'll have something going on the website if you guys are interested. Check out... we'll get something up on themanageher.com about that. For the woman in transition, what would you say to the woman who knows she's being called into a new chapter, but it doesn't make logical sense yet?

**Marcie:** The main thing is it's never going to make logical sense. And it's about taking one step at a time. And if you're hearing my story at all, then you know you're not alone. I felt very alone initially. And when I started to release some of holding on to past people, past scenarios, I was releasing those things, more and more doors opened up. And they'll open up at just the right time.

It can be scary when you don't know where things are going, but you feel like you should do this, just know that there are other people out there. And the more you're putting it out there that you need that connection and just looking and expecting to find support and guidance, it's there.

When you are going I don't know and you're living in that kind of fear state that's a normal human thing but fear can create that inaction and I feel like if you don't try to take on everything that you might be feeling you're meant to do and just baby step it just do one little thing put it out there connect with somebody share the topic that you're feeling called to share and get the encouragement that's out there. Because when... let's just say leaving corporate I know I've reached out within a big company to a lot of different people when I needed something strangers but still in the company. Now, when you're an entrepreneur or you're in this state of mind, you're out of that, the world is your company.

Realizing there's no harm and if you hear no, that's okay. Try put yourself out there and don't be afraid of the rejection because again, you miss how many shots? The ones you don't take, right?

**Aimee:** That's right. You miss every shot you don't take. That's right, Marcie. Well, Marcie, you're the best. This was such an interesting conversation because it really lives at the intersection of so many things we care about on this show. Reinvention, healing, courage, creativity, and what it looks like to build a life that's more aligned even when it's less conventional.

What I appreciate about your story is that it reminds us that sometimes the most meaningful work does not come from following the expected path. Sometimes it comes from loss. Sometimes it comes from listening more deeply. And sometimes it comes from being willing to trust the thing that you cannot fully explain yet.

Thank you for sharing your story, your heart, and this chapter of your work with us. If you want to learn more about Marcie and explore her work, you can visit her website at electricblueunited.com and find her on Instagram at love evolve music. It's l-o-v music. She's also on Facebook under Love Evolve Music and Marcie Dean and on YouTube at Love Evolve Music and on LinkedIn. So, there are plenty of ways to explore her work and follow along with what she's building. We'll link everything in the show notes so you can go deeper in a way that feels right for you.

And as always, if this episode resonated with you, share it with another woman who may be in the middle of her own pivot, her own healing, and her own reinvention. Because sometimes the next version of you doesn't arrive in a straight line. Sometimes she arrives through disruption, courage, and learning to trust yourself in a whole new way. Thank you, ladies. I'll see you next week. And thank you, Marcie, for being here today.

**Marcie:** Thank you, Aimee. This has been awesome.
content:
What happens when your entire life explodes in the span of six months? For Marcie Dean, it meant losing her partner Jon in a drowning accident, getting laid off from her senior role at AWS, and watching her brother succumb to a fatal overdose. But from that devastating foundation of grief, something extraordinary emerged: a gift for creating frequency-based healing music that she never knew she possessed.

Marcie's story challenges everything we think we know about career pivots, healing, and the mysterious ways life redirects us toward our true purpose. As a former IBM executive and Creative Director at AWS, she was deeply embedded in the corporate tech world when tragedy struck on Father's Day weekend 2023. Her partner Jon drowned while saving his son from a rip current, leaving behind a promise that would prove prophetic: if anything happened to him, he would communicate through music.

When Everything Falls Apart

The months following Jon's death read like a catalog of loss. Marcie returned to work at AWS, trying to maintain some semblance of normalcy, only to be laid off shortly after. Then her brother died from an overdose, completing a trifecta of devastation that would have crushed most people. Instead, it became the catalyst for Marcie's most important work.

Grief, as Marcie learned, isn't just about losing people. It's about losing your career identity, your relationships, the version of life you thought you were building. When everything familiar disappears, you're forced to discover what remains at your core. For Marcie, what remained was an inexplicable ability to create music that heals.

With no musical training beyond childhood piano lessons, Marcie opened GarageBand and began creating tracks that seemed to flow through her rather than from her. Two years and over 400 tracks later, she's built LovEvolve Music, a company pioneering what she calls Spirit Tech - a proprietary frequency-based audio system designed to regulate the nervous system and support healing.

The Science Behind Sound Healing

Marcie's approach isn't just spiritual - it's grounded in the understanding that our digital world has disconnected us from the natural frequencies our bodies need to heal. Every person has a unique frequency signature, much like a fingerprint, and her custom tracks are designed to meet your nervous system exactly where it is.

The results speak for themselves. Marcie shares the story of an autistic teenager whose first articulated words came after listening to her music. Parents report that children who struggled with traditional therapy methods respond dramatically to her frequency-based tracks. This isn't about replacing medical treatment - it's about addressing the nervous system regulation that must happen before other healing modalities can take hold.

As Marcie explains, mindset work alone cannot override a chronically dysregulated nervous system. You can tell yourself to think positive thoughts all day long, but if your nervous system is stuck in fight-or-flight mode, those affirmations won't penetrate. The frequency music creates the physiological foundation that allows other healing work to be effective.

The Entrepreneur's Journey

Becoming an accidental entrepreneur while processing profound grief isn't a path anyone would choose, yet Marcie's story illuminates something crucial about following your calling even when it doesn't make logical sense. She didn't leave corporate to start a sound healing business because she had a five-year plan. She followed the signs, the synchronicities, and the undeniable evidence that something greater was working through her.

The transition wasn't without challenges. The hardest part of leaving corporate, Marcie reveals, wasn't the financial uncertainty or even the identity shift - it was learning to surrender control. In corporate environments, you can strategize, analyze, and execute your way to success. Building a business around channeled healing music requires a completely different relationship with uncertainty and trust.

Her advice to women feeling called to a new chapter is profound in its simplicity: surrender is not giving up, it's stopping the fight against what already is. Like childbirth, the more you resist the process, the more painful it becomes. When you align with what's trying to emerge, the path opens up in ways you couldn't have planned or predicted.

Practical Tools for Modern Entrepreneurs

Despite her spiritual approach to business, Marcie remains grounded in practical systems and tools. She recommends AI platforms like Keeper for financial management, Figma Make for automation, and Claude for content creation. The key is finding technology that supports your mission rather than overwhelming your nervous system with more digital noise.

Her Choose Your Own Healing Journey platform allows people to experience customized frequency music based on their specific needs, whether they're processing grief, supporting a neurodivergent child, or simply seeking nervous system regulation in our chaotic world.

The Bigger Message

Marcie's story isn't just about one woman's journey from corporate executive to sound healer. It's about recognizing that our greatest gifts often emerge from our greatest challenges, that healing happens on multiple levels simultaneously, and that sometimes the most important work we'll do in the world looks nothing like what we trained for in school or built in our previous careers.

Her experience reminds us that when life dismantles everything we thought we knew about ourselves, it might not be destruction - it might be reconstruction. The frequency music that flows through Marcie now exists because she was willing to stay open to possibilities that her logical mind couldn't have conceived. In a world that often feels increasingly disconnected from natural rhythms and healing frequencies, her work offers a bridge back to the vibrational medicine our bodies innately understand.
